article.title: Google Splits Its Eighth-Generation TPU Into Separate Training and Inference Chips at Cloud Next 2026
article.summary: Google unveiled TPU 8t and TPU 8i at Cloud Next 2026, a dual-chip strategy claiming 2.8x faster training and 80% better inference price-performance over Ironwood, backed by a new Virgo Network fabric linking up to 1 million TPUs.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Google used the opening day of Cloud Next 2026 in Las Vegas to unveil its eighth-generation Tensor Processing Unit, splitting the line for the first time into two purpose-built chips: **TPU 8t** for training and **TPU 8i** for inference. The announcement on April 22 frames the change as a response to what the company describes as the "agentic era," where workloads are bifurcating into large pretraining runs on one side and latency-sensitive agent serving on the other. Google is pairing the silicon with a new data center fabric called Virgo Network and claims it can now lash more than a million TPUs together across multiple sites.

## What We Know

TPU 8t is Google's first generation to be fully decoupled from the inference path, a deliberate divergence from the unified design philosophy that defined prior generations including Ironwood (TPU v7). According to [Google's official announcement](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/infrastructure-and-cloud/google-cloud/eighth-generation-tpu-agentic-era/), a single TPU 8t superpod scales to 9,600 chips sharing 2 petabytes of memory and delivers 121 ExaFlops of compute, with "nearly 3x the compute performance per pod" compared to the previous generation. Google says the system is engineered to sustain greater than 97% "goodput" — productive compute time net of stalls and failures — by improving networking and reliability rather than raw peak throughput alone.

The sibling TPU 8i is explicitly optimized for inference and reinforcement-learning rollouts. Google's post describes 288 GB of high-bandwidth memory paired with 384 MB of on-chip SRAM, the latter figure representing a tripling over Ironwood, along with 19.2 Tb/s of interconnect bandwidth. The company claims "80% better performance-per-dollar" for inference workloads versus the prior generation, and says the chip can serve nearly twice the customer volume at the same cost. As [detailed by SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/22/google-unveils-new-tpus-power-next-wave-ai-training-inference/), TPU 8i replaces the SparseCore accelerator found in Ironwood with a new Collective Acceleration Engine that Google says cuts communication latency by a factor of five during autoregressive decoding.

The networking story sits alongside the silicon. Google's [infrastructure post for Next 26](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/compute/ai-infrastructure-at-next26) introduces Virgo Network, a megascale fabric with four times the bandwidth of previous generations that can connect 134,000 TPUs inside a single data center and extend to more than one million TPUs across multiple sites when orchestrated by Google's Pathways and JAX stacks. The same fabric carries 80,000 GPUs per data center in the new A5X bare-metal instances, which are based on Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL72 systems.

Beyond chips, Google announced Managed Lustre storage reaching 10 TB/s of bandwidth — a tenfold jump year over year — and new Z4M compute instances offering 168 TiB of local SSD per machine, aimed at large-scale data preparation for training. A companion GKE update promises four-times-faster node startup, 80% lower pod startup times, and a 70% reduction in time-to-first-token latency when using its new AI-powered Inference Gateway, [according to the same Google Cloud blog post](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/compute/ai-infrastructure-at-next26).

Sundar Pichai disclosed business-side figures in [his Cloud Next keynote summary](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/infrastructure-and-cloud/google-cloud/cloud-next-2026-sundar-pichai/), reporting that Google's first-party models now process more than 16 billion tokens per minute through direct customer API calls, up from 10 billion last quarter. He also said just over half of Google's 2026 machine learning compute investment will be allocated to Cloud customers and partners rather than internal use.

## What We Don't Know

Google has not published a firm availability date for either chip. The official announcement says only that they will be "generally available later this year," and press coverage at the event did not extract a narrower window. The company named **Citadel Securities** as a TPU 8 customer but stopped short of disclosing additional named early adopters, and no enterprise customers were attached to the chips in [TechCrunch's reporting](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/22/google-cloud-next-new-tpu-ai-chips-compete-with-nvidia/). Pricing for either SKU was not disclosed; all comparisons are expressed as performance-per-dollar ratios versus Ironwood rather than absolute rack or instance costs.

It is also unclear how Google's claimed gains translate against Nvidia's latest accelerators in independent benchmarks. [TechCrunch noted](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/22/google-cloud-next-new-tpu-ai-chips-compete-with-nvidia/) that Google is not trying to replace Nvidia in its own cloud — the company is simultaneously launching Vera Rubin-based A5X instances and collaborating with Nvidia on the Falcon networking protocol — and analyst Patrick Moorhead is quoted pointing out that predictions of TPU displacing Nvidia have a decade-long track record of not materializing. [The Register similarly observed](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/22/google_tpu8_dual_track_training_inference/) that a single Nvidia Rubin GPU at 35 petaFLOPS still outperforms an individual TPU 8t chip on peak compute, though the argument Google makes is about fabric-level scaling rather than per-chip throughput.

## Analysis

The split between TPU 8t and TPU 8i is the clearest signal yet that Google sees training and inference as fundamentally different businesses with different economic profiles. Training remains a campaign-style workload where customers are willing to tolerate complexity in exchange for time-to-result on a frontier model, which favors giant synchronous pods and the 3D torus topology of TPU 8t. Inference at agent scale, by contrast, lives or dies on tail latency and cost per token, and that is the gap the dedicated 8i chip — with tripled SRAM, the Collective Acceleration Engine, and the 1,152-chip Boardfly pod — is designed to close.

The approach mirrors the AWS Graviton/Trainium model of narrowing compute to the shape of the workload, and it echoes Google's broader effort at Cloud Next to position itself as the default substrate for agent platforms. That context matters: in the same keynote Google launched the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and committed $750 million to partner incentives, and it reports 40% quarter-over-quarter growth in paid monthly active users for Gemini Enterprise. If those agents materialize at the volumes Google is projecting, cheaper inference silicon becomes a cost-of-goods lever as much as a technical milestone.

The ambition of the fabric story — one million TPUs orchestrated across multiple data centers as a single cluster — is also a concession that scaling laws have pushed past the boundary of any individual facility. That is consistent with broader industry signals: the announcement lands the same week that [Kubernetes v1.36 graduated its Dynamic Resource Allocation features](/article/2026-04/21-kubernetes-v136-arrives-with-security-hardening-selinux-acceleration-and-expanded-ai-hardware-support) precisely to handle this class of accelerator-heavy, cross-cluster workload. Whether Google's Virgo fabric delivers the near-linear efficiency it promises at a million-chip scale will be the story to watch once third parties can run their own benchmarks.
